!! I Reverse-Engineer Chaos Into Clarity

After 28 years building and rescuing enterprise systems, I've learned that the best solutions aren't always the obvious ones—they're the elegant shortcuts that work properly.

!!! Legacy System Rescue

Forensic reverse-engineering of "black box" systems. Making undocumented legacy systems work when documentation doesn't exist.

!!! Cross-Platform Integration

Building bridges between systems that weren't designed to talk. SCORM, xAPI, AICC protocol expertise.

!!! AI Implementation

Thoughtful AI/LLM application. Understanding when to use generative AI vs. structured data for production systems.

!!! Systems Architecture

Building foundational systems that harmonize—like the diapason register that gives a pipe organ its character. 28+ Years 5,200+ Projects 40+ Clients 930+ Testimonials

!! Five Core Influences

Not abstract inspirations—specific sources that shaped how I solve problems. Full details → 1. Air Crash Investigation Applied to the GM course completion investigation : a 4x spike in incomplete rates traced to fragile LMS architecture, misleading navigation, and mismatched real-world usage patterns. 2. Speedrunning / TASBot Applied to build a SCORM debugging tool that reduced QA cycles from hours to minutes using emulator save-state methodology. 3. “You want to cheat, cheat fair—anything I hate is a crookin’ crook.” —Moe Howard, “Healthy, Wealthy and Dumb” (1938). The source of the tagline. If you’ve found an elegant shortcut, document it, share it, build it properly. Hoarding a clever solution is the real dishonesty. Full philosophy → 4. Mentour Pilot The model for AI as a force multiplier: domain expert + AI assistant = better analysis than either alone. 5. “I seek knowledge not for gain, but to better understand myself” A line from a Harry Potter fanfic that stuck. The motivation behind every deep investigation: understanding the system, not padding the resume.

!! Featured Projects

⚠ FINDING 1

!!! Cross-Domain SCORM Framework

2011-2016 | Status: 10+ years in production

!!!! Analysis

Energy utilities needed cross-domain SCORM delivery before browsers or LMS vendors supported it. Same-origin policy blocked the obvious solutions.

!!!! Solution

Engineered cross-domain communication using EasyXDM. Built SCORM API proxy for transparent AICC/SCORM protocol translation. Created automated package generation tooling.

!!!! Outcome

System served ~20 energy utilities (each drawing a curated subset from a ~2,000 course central library). Remained in production 10+ years. Built in 2011—five years before Rustici released Content Controller. Full investigation report → JavaScript EasyXDM SCORM AICC Groovy ⚠ FINDING 2

!!! Legacy Courseware CMS Rescue

2004-2016 | Status: Extended system lifespan 10+ years

!!!! Analysis

Inherited undocumented courseware CMS on Macromedia JRun platform when original developer left. No documentation, no source code access, production system serving clients.

!!!! Solution

Decompiled Java/JSP packages to understand system behavior. Built JavaScript workarounds where server-side refactoring wasn't practical. Maintained critical system beyond expected EOL.

!!!! Outcome

System remained operational 10+ years. Enabled company to continue serving clients while planning proper migration path. Java/JSP JavaScript Macromedia JRun Forensic Engineering ⚠ FINDING 3

!!! Enterprise AI Training Agent

2025-2026 | Status: Production

!!!! Analysis

Needed AI instructor agent in Copilot Studio. Prompt engineering alone couldn't establish reliable guardrails for progress tracking and navigation.

!!!! Solution

Built data pipeline converting eLearning course materials into structured JSON. Used AI code interpreter for content transformation, structured data for deterministic control. Created adaptive cards for navigation and assessments.

!!!! Outcome

Reliable course navigation and progress tracking. Demonstrated hybrid AI/structured data architecture pattern. Full investigation report → Microsoft Copilot Studio Adaptive Cards AI Code Interpreter JSON

!! From the Field

Direct feedback from engagements spanning 17 years Dan’s technical expertise is tremendous… with his help, we were able to solve two large technical issues we were having, one that will have a direct impact on the Flash conversion process and save a lot of time and money. This resulted in a savings of about 600 hours of labor by allowing us to publish large batches of lessons unattended. title: Philosophy
text:
How I think about engineering problems

!! Pattern 158

Near the end of Myst, the brothers Sirrus and Achenar whisper a clue through their prison books: “The final page is in the fireplace... Pattern 158.” You find the pattern book in the library, enter the code on the fireplace grid, and a wall rotates open to reveal a hidden chamber. It works. You've solved the puzzle. Except you haven't. The brothers are manipulating you. They want you to retrieve their final pages so they can escape. The obvious answer — the red and blue pages — is a trap. The real solution is the green book sitting right next to them. Their father Atrus is inside it. He knows what they did. And the entire puzzle was designed to stop you from seeing that. Pattern 158 = the code that reveals both the trap and the true solution. That's how I approach engineering problems. The obvious answer is often a trap. The real problem is usually sitting right next to the stated problem — you have to see through the framing to find it. Every forensic investigation I've run has had this shape: what the client reported and what was actually happening were related, but not the same thing.

!! This Is What Design Thinking Looks Like When It's a Personality, Not a Process

Design thinking was invented to teach people who don't naturally do certain things to do them systematically. Empathize before you solve. Define the real problem before you build. Iterate through failure without shame. I didn't learn this from a framework. I absorbed it from air crash investigators, speedrunners, and a Three Stooges short from 1938. The result is the same disposition — but without the scaffolding, because the scaffolding was never load-bearing for me. The fish doesn't have a water strategy. What I do have is a three-step pattern that has repeated across every significant project in 28 years:

!!! 1. Deconstruct the Chaos

Forensic engineering before solution engineering. Understand the actual system — not the documented one, not the stated one, not the one someone remembers from three years ago. The real one, running in production, behaving the way it behaves. Never blame the human first. When a system fails, the system designed the conditions for that failure. The Swiss cheese model: disasters require contributing factors to align. The person who clicked the wrong button is almost never the root cause. This is empathy with teeth. It's not about being charitable — it's about being accurate.

!!! 2. Build the Tool

Once you understand the real problem, don't fix the instance. Fix the category. A one-time fix is technical debt in disguise. A tool is a permanent reduction in the problem surface. The SCORM Debugger didn't fix one testing cycle — it eliminated hours of manual clicking from every testing cycle, permanently, for everyone who used it. The cross-domain SCORM framework didn't solve one client's delivery problem — it solved the structural impossibility that was blocking all cross-domain delivery, five years before the vendor community caught up. The goal is always the tool, never the ticket.

!!! 3. Empower the User

A tool that requires its creator to operate it is a dependency, not a solution. Everything I build is documented, maintainable, and designed to outlast my involvement. This is the hardest part to get right, and the part most engineers skip. You haven't solved the problem until someone else can own the solution.

!! The Moral Spine

The three-step pattern is how I work. This is why : “You want to cheat, cheat fair — anything I hate is a crookin' crook.” — Moe Howard, Healthy, Wealthy and Dumb , 1938 Every tool I build is a cheat. Automating a manual process is cheating. Jumping to any course state in seconds instead of clicking through every section is cheating. Building a framework that eliminates a class of problem is cheating. But cheating fair means the output is proper, documented, and maintainable. It means you could hand it to another engineer and they could understand, extend, and improve it. Hoarding a clever solution — keeping the elegant shortcut to yourself — is the real dishonesty. The knowledge isn't yours. It came from everyone who documented the problem before you, and it belongs to everyone who'll encounter it after you. Clever shortcuts and corner-cutting look identical from the outside. The difference is in the craftsmanship.

!! Five Sources That Shaped the Method

These aren't abstract inspirations. They're specific sources I can point to and say: this changed how I solve problems.

!!! Air Crash Investigation

Sources: Mayday / Air Crash Investigation (TV), Mentour Pilot (YouTube), 74 Gear (YouTube) The NTSB doesn’t arrive at a crash site and blame the pilot. They reconstruct the full causal chain — what the system allowed, what the design encouraged, what the training didn’t cover, what the weather added. The finding is almost never a single cause. It’s a pattern of contributing factors that aligned. I run every significant investigation the same way. The GM course completion spike — a 4x increase in incomplete rates across a major client — looked like a tracking bug. It wasn’t. Technical forensics (Fiddler, browser debugging), UX analysis, and user interviews revealed three contributing factors: fragile architecture, confusing navigation, and sales reps taking training on poor WiFi between customer meetings. All three had to align for the failure to occur. Fix only one, and you’ve addressed a symptom. The method: reconstruct before you diagnose. Diagnose before you fix.

!!! Speedrunning / TASBot Culture

Sources: TASBot demonstrations, tool-assisted speedruns, emulator save states A Tool-Assisted Speedrun uses emulator save states and frame-perfect input to understand a game at a level no human can reach in real time. It’s not cheating in the pejorative sense — it’s systematic mastery. You understand the rules so completely that you can build tools that operate beyond what the rules were designed to allow. The SCORM Debugger is TASBot for eLearning. Testing a gated course meant manually advancing through every section to reach the one you needed to test. Hours per cycle. I built a Vue.js wrapper with a fake SCORM API: save course state at any point, restore it instantly, edit SCORM values directly, jump anywhere. Testing cycles dropped from hours to seconds. The methodology is identical — save state, manipulate, replay, optimize.

!!! “I Seek Knowledge Not for Gain, But to Better Understand Myself”

Sources: Harry Potter fanfic — password to the Department of Mysteries I don’t reverse-engineer a legacy CMS to fix bugs. I do it to understand the mind of the engineer who built it. I don’t study SCORM specifications to bill more hours. I study them to understand why LMS vendors interpret the same spec differently — what assumptions were baked into each implementation, what the authors of the spec meant vs. what the market heard. The knowledge isn’t the goal. The understanding is. And the understanding of a system is inseparable from the understanding of the people who built it, used it, and broke it.

!!! Mentour Pilot’s AI Application

Sources: ~2022–2023, commercial airline pilot building an AI assistant for aviation procedures A domain expert using AI as a force multiplier — not AI hype, but practical utility. His expertise became more accessible and more useful because he understood both the domain and the tool’s actual capabilities. This was the seed for how I approach AI implementation. Domain knowledge isn’t replaceable by AI. AI is replaceable by better AI. The combination — expert judgment augmented by systematic analysis — is what produces results. The Power Platform engagement: undefined requirements, departed vendors, three weeks to specification. AI as a pressure-testing partner, not an answer machine. GitHub Spec Kit emerged from that model.

!! On AI and This Site

The same philosophy applies to how this site was written. I write in streams of consciousness. What comes out is tangled, recursive, full of half-finished thoughts that loop back on themselves. It's accurate. It's complete. It's also exhausting to read. For years, the solution was to agonize — wordsmith the sentence, spend twenty minutes sounding “professional,” which usually meant sounding like everyone else: fine, forgettable, friction-filled to produce. I brain-dump now, hand it to an AI, and review what comes back. The ideas are mine. The AI's job is to make it readable — to do what a good editor does, which is get out of the way of the meaning. This isn't laziness. It's the same philosophy I apply to code. Good code is self-documenting. When I inherit a codebase that requires a novel of inline comments to navigate, that's a smell — the code is failing to communicate. Professional writing has the same failure mode. The friction of converting thought to readable prose was never adding value to the thinking itself. “AI wrote it” is a mischaracterization. AI clarified it. The thinking, the investigation, the conclusions — those are mine. What I'm outsourcing is the translation friction. My prime directive is clarity. If an AI can take a tangled first draft and make it immediately readable — and I can review it, own it, and stand behind every word — the communication succeeded. The question isn't “did AI touch this.” The question is: does the reader understand what I meant to say? Much of this site was built with AI assistance — content, code, and design. The thinking behind it is mine. The clarity is the point. That's Provider of Clarity applied to its own medium.

!! The Six Brand Elements

Six patterns extracted from 28 years of actual work — not marketing slogans, but recurring themes from real projects. 1. Provider of Clarity The outcome. Enter chaos, deliver understanding. Legacy systems, undefined requirements, failing courses — hand Dan a mess, get back clarity. 2. I Cheat, But I Cheat Fair The method. Build tools that skip tedium through proper engineering — documented, maintainable, ethical optimization. The Three Stooges, “Healthy, Wealthy and Dumb,” 1938 3. Don’t Fix the Bug, Build the Tool The approach. Don’t solve individual problems; prevent entire categories of problems. 4. I Seek Knowledge Not for Gain, But to Better Understand Myself The motivation. Learn to understand systems and your own thinking, not to pad a resume. Harry Potter fanfic — password to the Department of Mysteries 5. The Machine to Undo Time After Disaster The role. Tier 3 escalation — forensic engineering to understand what went wrong, then build the tool that prevents it again. Same fanfic — the artifact guarded behind the password 6. TASBot for [Domain] The creative application. Tool-assisted optimization applied to professional work — understand the rules deeply, then build tools that operate beyond manual precision. Tool-Assisted Speedrunning culture

!! What Colleagues Say

Thank you so much for Dan for putting time into thinking this through and writing it up. Program Manager, Microsoft Account GP Strategies — on a SCORM architecture analysis document Dan, thank you for being such a team player with the GP team and the client, and thank you for your incredible knowledge and expertise. Account Manager, GP Strategies Entergy engagement — on cross-team collaboration Pattern 158: I cheat, but I cheat fair.

title: Contact
text:
What I'm looking for — and what I'm not.

!! Where I Do My Best Work

I'm a senior individual contributor who builds things. Not a manager, not a consultant billing by the hour, not a contractor who disappears after the sprint.

!!! I'm looking for:

Senior or Staff Full-Stack Engineer — my primary lane. JavaScript is a 28-year daily driver. Vue.js, React, TypeScript, Node.js, SQL in production. I build the frontend, wire up the backend, and debug the space between them. Solutions Architect — my signature pattern. I design systems that make incompatible platforms talk to each other. CSBB Dispatch ran for 10+ years across Fortune 500 energy clients. BP's federated frontend stitched React and GraphQL across four backend systems that were never designed to coexist. Senior Frontend Engineer at enterprise scale — Material UI theming, Tailwind CSS, WCAG 2.1 / Section 508 accessibility with JAWS and NVDA. I've built production interfaces for HSBC, PNC Bank, and TD Bank.

!!! I'm not looking for:

Management or non-coding roles eLearning / Instructional Design (that chapter is closed) .NET-primary, WordPress, or mobile-only stacks QA-only or DevOps-only roles Anything where "senior" means "writes tickets for juniors"

!! Where I Fit Best

Product company over consultancy. I spent 28.5 years at a consultancy. I know the model. I'm looking for a company that ships a product and stands behind it — where engineering quality is a business advantage, not a line item someone's trying to minimize. Mid-size — roughly 100 to 1,000 people. Big enough for interesting integration problems and competitive compensation. Small enough that a senior IC with my breadth has visible impact and isn't anonymized into a team of 200. Late-stage startup to established mid-market. Startup energy with healthy processes. Not a 10-person seed stage burning runway, not a Fortune 100 where changing a button color requires three committees. Domain complexity is a plus. I've shipped production software for banking (HSBC, PNC, TD Bank, Wells Fargo), defense (General Dynamics Electric Boat), energy (CSBB across 10+ utilities), and healthcare. Regulated industries with real constraints are where my forensic methodology pays off — not a liability.

!! What Matters

Remote-first, or remote-real. Not "remote but everyone who gets promoted is in the office." I'm in Portland, Oregon, and this is non-negotiable. Hybrid within the Portland/Vancouver WA metro is fine. Autonomy for senior engineers. I managed myself across 24+ Fortune 500 client engagements over nearly three decades. I don't need a standup to know what to do next. I need context, access, and trust. Craftsmanship over velocity. "Quality over speed" isn't a platitude for me — it's how I've worked my entire career. I build things that last. CSBB Dispatch ran 10+ years in production. The systems I leave behind don't need me to survive. I want a team that cares about getting it right, not just getting it shipped. Evidence over credentials. I don't have a four-year degree. I don't have cloud certifications. What I have is 14 documented case studies, 28 years of production code, and a portfolio backed by 34,650 analyzed emails. I need a hiring process that can evaluate demonstrated work — not one that filters on checkboxes. No leetcode. I evaluate companies the same way I evaluate systems: by their design. A hiring process built on algorithmic puzzles tells me the company optimizes for proximate signals over actual engineering ability. I'm happy to do take-home projects, pair on real problems, or walk through my portfolio. I'm not happy to invert a binary tree on a whiteboard. Rising tide. I build tools that make the whole team better — the SCORM Debugger, the Xyleme automation suite, build pipeline tooling. I want teammates who think the same way. Collaborative, not competitive. "We shipped it" over "I shipped it."

!! The Numbers

Compensation and location details Employment Full-time preferred Base salary $140,000 - $250,000 Floor $120,000 (non-negotiable) Contract rate $60/hour minimum Location Remote (preferred) or hybrid Portland metro Timezone US Pacific (PT)

!! Let's Talk

If this sounds like your team, I'd like to hear from you. Email dan@pattern158.solutions Copy LinkedIn linkedin.com/in/pattern158 GitHub github.com/novakda Case Files pattern158.solutions/case-files When reaching out, the most useful thing you can include is: what does the role actually look like day-to-day? Job descriptions are marketing. I want to know what I'd be building, who I'd be building it with, and what problem the team is trying to solve. Dan, thank you for being such a team player with the GP team and the client, and thank you for your incredible knowledge and expertise. Account Manager, GP Strategies You have helped us move the bar to better, more accessible courses and helped me learn a ton. Program Manager, Microsoft Account GP Strategies — Microsoft accessibility initiative

title: Accessibility
text:
Pattern 158 Solutions is committed to ensuring digital accessibility for all users.

!! Commitment to Accessibility

Pattern 158 Solutions is dedicated to making this website accessible to the widest possible audience, including individuals with disabilities. We strive to conform to the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.1 Level AA standard.

!! Standards and Conformance

This website has been designed and tested to meet the following accessibility standards: WCAG 2.1 Level AA — Web Content Accessibility Guidelines version 2.1, conformance level AA Semantic HTML5 — Proper use of landmark elements, heading hierarchy, and ARIA attributes Keyboard Navigation — All functionality accessible via keyboard without mouse Screen Reader Compatibility — Tested for compatibility with common assistive technologies

!! Accessibility Testing

We employ multiple testing methodologies to ensure accessibility compliance: Automated Testing axe-core automated accessibility engine running on all 22 pages in both light and dark themes. Tests cover WCAG 2.1 Level A and AA success criteria. Manual Testing Keyboard navigation testing of all interactive elements. Visual inspection of color contrast, heading hierarchy, and semantic structure. Cross-Browser Validation Functional testing on Chromium-based browsers (Chrome, Edge) and Firefox to ensure consistent experience across browsers. Viewport Testing Responsive design validated at desktop (1200px) and mobile (375px) viewports with full screenshot evidence.

!! Current Status

Last Verified: February 21, 2026 Test Results: Dark mode: 21/21 pages pass all WCAG 2.1 AA automated tests (100% compliance) Light mode: Substantially compliant with minor cosmetic exceptions Semantic structure: 100% compliant across all pages Keyboard navigation: 100% compliant across all pages Cross-browser: Validated on Chromium and Firefox

!! Browsers and Assistive Technologies

This website has been tested with the following browsers: Chrome (latest version) Microsoft Edge (latest version) Firefox (latest version) The site is designed to work with assistive technologies including: Screen readers (NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver) Keyboard-only navigation Voice control software Browser zoom and text resizing up to 200%

!! Accessibility Features

This website includes the following accessibility features: Skip to main content link at the top of every page for keyboard users Semantic HTML landmarks (header, nav, main, footer) for screen reader navigation Proper heading hierarchy (h1 → h2 → h3) with no skipped levels Alternative text for all meaningful images High contrast ratios between text and background (minimum 4.5:1 for normal text) Visible focus indicators for keyboard navigation (4.5:1 contrast ratio) Dark mode support respecting system preferences and manual toggle Responsive design that works on mobile devices and tablets Clear link purpose with descriptive link text (no "click here") Consistent navigation across all pages

!! Known Issues

We continuously work to improve accessibility. Currently identified issues include: Some decorative badge elements on the Technologies page and select exhibits have color contrast slightly below 4.5:1 in light mode. These are cosmetic elements that do not impact access to core content. These issues are documented and scheduled for remediation.

!! Feedback and Contact

We welcome feedback on the accessibility of Pattern 158 Solutions. If you encounter accessibility barriers on this website, please contact us: Email: dan@pattern158.solutions Contact Page: Contact Dan Novak We aim to respond to accessibility feedback within 5 business days.

!! Technical Specifications

This website's accessibility relies on the following technologies: HTML5 CSS3 JavaScript (for theme toggle and navigation; core content accessible without JavaScript) ARIA (Accessible Rich Internet Applications) attributes for enhanced semantics

text:
! GM Learning Portal: Course Completion Anomaly Investigation
''Summary:'' Investigated 19% course incompletion rate (4x baseline) initially framed as tracking bug. Multi-angle investigation revealed five concurrent systemic failures: memory cache vulnerability with no auto-save, "Congratulations You Failed" UX pattern, confusing navigation, mobile workforce on poor WiFi, and short-burst usage pattern conflicting with hour-long desktop design. Platform eventually abandoned — indirect validation of findings.
''Role:'' NTSB-style forensic analysis — systems thinking over code debugging
''Date:'' 2017–2018
''Email count:'' 618 emails

! Investigation Summary

GM reported a 4x spike in incomplete courses after migrating to a new LMS. Initial assumption: tracking bug. Multi-angle forensic investigation — technical forensics, UX analysis, and user research — identified five concurrent systemic failures aligned in a “Swiss cheese model.” The root cause wasn’t a single bug; it was a systemic mismatch between how the platform was designed and how people actually worked.

Building on a decade of GP Strategies’ embedded work on Microsoft accounts, Dan was brought in to evolve their AI training agent in Copilot Studio. Zero platform experience to functional POC in ~4 weeks. The breakthrough: separating deterministic state (structured JSON) from AI interaction (content delivery), creating a hybrid architecture that solved the reliability challenges of the original declarative approach.

title: A Finding: SCORM courses dependent on Cornerstone Network Player
text:
! Finding

SCORM courses dependent on Cornerstone Network Player

! Description

SCORM requires a player to communicate between the course and the LMS. If the Cornerstone Network Player went down, all SCORM courses stopped functioning. AICC was used as a workaround but had significant limitations.

! Resolution

Dan provided a cross-domain SCORM wrapper that eliminates dependency on the Cornerstone Network Player entirely. A SCORM 2004 version was prototyped within one week of the on-site visit.

title: A Finding: HTML5 courses failing under AICC protocol
text:
! Finding

HTML5 courses failing under AICC protocol

! Description

HTML5 is the modern publishing format replacing Flash. EB's HTML5 AICC courses threw errors at launch and could not communicate with the LMS, preventing completion recording.

! Resolution

Converted AICC courses to SCORM HTML5 using the cross-domain wrapper. Courses performed better than they had under the previous AICC configuration.

title: A Finding: Quiz bookmarking and reset failures
text:
! Finding

Quiz bookmarking and reset failures

! Description

AICC could not properly reset quizzes for multiple attempts. Bookmarking was unreliable, limiting course design options for EB's curriculum development team.

! Resolution

SCORM HTML5 with the cross-domain wrapper properly supports bookmarking, reset, and multiple quiz attempts. Curriculum developers gained new design freedom previously unavailable under AICC constraints.

title: A Finding: No tools to verify SCORM data flow
text:
! Finding

No tools to verify SCORM data flow

! Description

EB's IT department was disabling troubleshooting tools (Inspect Element) on workstations. There was no way to observe data moving between courses and the LMS during testing or debugging.

! Resolution

Dan provided an LMS simulation tool that captures and displays all course-to-LMS communication. The tool was also usable for training EB staff on SCORM data concepts and troubleshooting methodology.

title: A Finding: Bulk SCORM import not available
text:
! Finding

Bulk SCORM import not available

! Description

Cornerstone only allowed single-course uploads. GP's enterprise course library (1,000+ courses) could not be practically migrated to EB's LMS using the one-at-a-time upload process.

! Resolution

The team tested Cornerstone's new bulk Course Publication tool with 7 SCORM courses from the enterprise course library. Successfully validated the migration path for future large-scale course library deployment.

title: D Finding: Legacy format incompatibilities
text:
! Finding

Legacy format incompatibilities

! Description

Courses authored in Adobe Presenter and standalone HTML5 required significant structural rework to map into Xyleme's topic-based authoring model. Flash modules needed careful handling to ensure ActionScript interactions functioned when wrapped in Xyleme's SCORM publishing layer.

! Resolution

Structural rework completed — courses successfully mapped into Xyleme's topic-based model

title: D Finding: IE runtime environment failures
text:
! Finding

IE runtime environment failures

! Description

Wells Fargo's corporate browser standard was Internet Explorer, which introduced SCORM API communication quirks. Flash-based content exhibited completion tracking failures from a race condition in IE's COM SCORM adapter, exacerbated by Xyleme's thin package latency.

! Resolution

Root-caused to race condition in IE's COM SCORM adapter; fix deployed across Flash course portfolio

title: D Finding: High-volume delivery cadence
text:
! Finding

High-volume delivery cadence

! Description

100+ courses in scope over a six-month timeline required a reliable, repeatable QA process. Dan's interactivity-level-based estimation methodology provided the predictability needed to schedule testing across the full portfolio.

! Resolution

Interactivity-level estimation methodology adopted for effort estimation across full portfolio

title: D Finding: Client relationship pressure
text:
! Finding

Client relationship pressure

! Description

Wells Fargo was explicitly identified as a critical account. Any delays in testing turnaround would cascade into missed course launch deadlines, potentially damaging the strategic relationship.

! Resolution

Testing schedule maintained despite obstacles; client independently relayed appreciation to GP leadership

title: E Finding: Cross-domain architecture
text:
! Finding

Cross-domain architecture

! Description

EasyXDM-based communication layer bridged same-origin policy constraints between GP content servers and client LMS domains — transparent to both content and LMS

title: E Finding: Protocol agnostic
text:
! Finding

Protocol agnostic

! Description

SCORM API proxy provided transparent AICC/SCORM protocol translation — identical content packages worked across LMS platforms regardless of protocol dialect

title: E Finding: Deployment revolution
text:
! Finding

Deployment revolution

! Description

Stub packages (kilobytes) replaced full content packages (megabytes) on client LMS. Course updates deployed centrally, propagated instantly to all clients

title: E Finding: Automation at scale
text:
! Finding

Automation at scale

! Description

CSBB Generator tooling (Groovy, Access, Windows Forms) automated package creation for a ~2,000 course library across ~20 clients — global deployments without touching individual systems

title: E Finding: Cross-industry reuse
text:
! Finding

Cross-industry reuse

! Description

Architecture originally built for energy utilities was successfully adapted for defense sector (General Dynamics Electric Boat) — proving the pattern was reusable, not domain-specific

title: F Finding: SCORM API wrapper bug
text:
! Finding

SCORM API wrapper bug

! Description

The SCORM API wrapper used across HSBC courseware contained a hardcoded exit value that conflicted with SuccessFactors' completion detection logic. Silent failure — no console errors, no visible indication — persisted undetected across multiple LMS migrations.

! Resolution

Incorrect exit value fixed; completion tracking restored across affected courses

title: F Finding: Lost Flash source files
text:
! Finding

Lost Flash source files

! Description

Legacy Flash-based courses needed updates but original FLA source files had been lost through vendor project handoffs. Required forensic decompilation of published SWF files to recover assets, ActionScript code, and timeline data.

! Resolution

SWF files forensically decompiled to recover source content; assets preserved for future maintenance

title: F Finding: Cross-region deployment inconsistencies
text:
! Finding

Cross-region deployment inconsistencies

! Description

Localized HTML5 courses failed to deploy correctly across HSBC's global SAP SuccessFactors instance spanning Mexico, India, and the Philippines. Diagnosis required navigating time zones, language barriers, and regional LMS configuration differences.

! Resolution

SCORM deployment issues diagnosed and fix coordinated across international stakeholders

title: H Finding: LMS configuration mismatch
text:
! Finding

LMS configuration mismatch

! Description

High-visibility additive manufacturing course was publishing SCORM completion data correctly, but the LMS was not configured to receive it in the expected format. Failure surfaced only in production after the course had already passed QA.

! Resolution

Root cause identified and fix deployed same business day without republishing course content

title: J Finding: Memory cache vulnerability
text:
! Finding

Memory cache vulnerability

! Description

The LMS held all course state in memory cache. Only saved to database via form submit when the course closed properly. No auto-save, no graceful degradation. Browser crash, tab close, or connection drop = all progress lost.

title: J Finding: "Congratulations You Failed" UX
text:
! Finding

"Congratulations You Failed" UX

! Description

Courses displayed a completion message after the final assessment — but that wasn't the last page. Users had to navigate to one more slide to trigger SCORM completion. The UI actively misled learners into closing before completion registered.

title: J Finding: Confusing navigation
text:
! Finding

Confusing navigation

! Description

The vendor's authoring tool produced courses with unintuitive navigation controls. Even expert users with deep courseware experience struggled to use the interface — average GM sales reps with no eLearning background had no chance.

title: J Finding: Mobile workforce / poor WiFi
text:
! Finding

Mobile workforce / poor WiFi

! Description

GM sales reps were always on the road, working on intermittent WiFi connections. The system had no recovery mechanism for connection drops, making every session a gamble.

title: J Finding: Short-burst usage pattern
text:
! Finding

Short-burst usage pattern

! Description

System was designed for hour-long desktop sessions. Actual users took training in 5-minute bursts between customer meetings. Each improper close triggered the cache vulnerability, dumping all progress.

title: K Finding: LLM used as data store
text:
! Finding

LLM used as data store

! Description

The original declarative agent architecture treated the LLM as both interface and data store. LLMs are probabilistic — they approximate, hallucinate, and lose context across conversation boundaries. For training applications requiring deterministic progress tracking, this was a fundamental architectural mistake.

title: K Finding: No separation of deterministic and probabilistic concerns
text:
! Finding

No separation of deterministic and probabilistic concerns

! Description

Course catalog, progress tracking, assessment scoring, and navigation paths were all handled through prompt engineering rather than structured data. No auto-save, no persisted state, no deterministic sequence enforcement.

title: L Finding: No data model
text:
! Finding

No data model

! Description

No fully defined data model existed. Dataverse tables were being created ad hoc during development without a schema design phase. Entity relationships, data types, and constraints were improvised.

title: L Finding: No version control
text:
! Finding

No version control

! Description

No git, no source control of any kind. The only method of versioning was solution exports kept by the lead developer. No rollback capability, no change tracking, no collaborative development safety net.

title: L Finding: Monolithic solution architecture
text:
! Finding

Monolithic solution architecture

! Description

One large solution for the entire system instead of a modular core with separate solutions per model-driven app. Deployment required manually identifying dependencies and copying components to a new fresh solution.

title: L Finding: Requirements atomization without context
text:
! Finding

Requirements atomization without context

! Description

Requirements degraded through PPT → Word → Excel → ADO conversion chain. Individual tickets looked actionable but had lost architectural context. Epic-level summaries treated as stories.

title: L Finding: No decomposed user stories
text:
! Finding

No decomposed user stories

! Description

No user stories with acceptance criteria. No definition of done. No way to verify whether built features matched business intent.

title: M Finding: QA time sink
text:
! Finding

QA time sink

! Description

Manual course navigation consumed hours per test cycle — multiply by hundreds of courses and dozens of test scenarios per course

title: M Finding: State management parallel
text:
! Finding

State management parallel

! Description

TASBot/speedrunning emulator save states enable instant reproduction of arbitrary game state — the same approach applied to SCORM session data

title: M Finding: Tool effectiveness
text:
! Finding

Tool effectiveness

! Description

State capture and restore reduced targeted test cycles from hours to minutes — any scenario reproducible instantly

title: M Finding: Adoption barrier
text:
! Finding

Adoption barrier

! Description

Direct-labor billing model made non-billable tool development economically invisible — the tool existed but was never formally deployed team-wide

title: M Finding: Rustici gap
text:
! Finding

Rustici gap

! Description

Rustici Engine's testing tools address package validation but lack reproducible debug states — the specific capability this tool provided

title: N Finding: Undocumented theming
text:
! Finding

Undocumented theming

! Description

Material UI component relationships were not documented — visual changes cascaded in unexpected ways, requiring archaeological investigation of the component tree

title: N Finding: Federated architecture
text:
! Finding

Federated architecture

! Description

The platform was a facade over four independent systems — no single system owned the learner record; cross-system state management was implicit

title: N Finding: Familiar pattern
text:
! Finding

Familiar pattern

! Description

The federated integration pattern matched CSBB Dispatch from 2011 — a decade of architectural evolution from custom-built to commercial ecosystem solving the same problem

title: N Finding: Scope estimation challenge
text:
! Finding

Scope estimation challenge

! Description

Rebranding scope masked underlying integration complexity — what appeared to be a theming engagement required cross-system debugging across all four backends

title: N Finding: Cross-system debugging
text:
! Finding

Cross-system debugging

! Description

Bugs required tracing across GraphQL queries, backend API responses, LRS xAPI statements, and Cognito authentication flows simultaneously

title: O Finding: Pattern recognition
text:
! Finding

Pattern recognition

! Description

Same architectural pattern across contexts: CSBB Dispatch (2011) → BP Learning Platform (2024) — federated integration layers making multiple systems behave as one. Dan recognized the 2024 version because he'd built his own in 2011

title: O Finding: Organic evolution
text:
! Finding

Organic evolution

! Description

Integration expertise naturally leading to AI product work — not deliberate career planning, but organic evolution where the skill thread was real

title: O Finding: Inherited code stewardship
text:
! Finding

Inherited code stewardship

! Description

AICPA Bridge Adapter demonstrated the ability to step into complex codebases built by others, becoming an L3 escalation point — system co-ownership without original authorship

title: O Finding: Design-to-code pragmatism
text:
! Finding

Design-to-code pragmatism

! Description

ContentAIQ required translating Adobe XD mockups from a graphic artist into interactive React components, navigating the gap between static visual design and functional UI implementation

title: O Finding: Cross-system debugging
text:
! Finding

Cross-system debugging

! Description

All three projects required tracing issues across system boundaries: GraphQL → backend services, webhook → database → REST API, frontend → AI pipeline

title: Testimonial: Manager, Content Team, GP Strategies
text:
<<<
Dan Novak “The Fiddler” (Automation) — Dan gets this name not only because he makes extensive use of the Fiddler application to help us troubleshoot communications errors for customers, but because he fiddles with stuff until we get a tool that makes things… possible. He also used Fiddler to figure out how to automate the publishing process out of Xyleme. This resulted in a savings of about 600 hours of labor by allowing us to publish large batches of lessons unattended.
<<<

''— Manager, Content Team, GP Strategies''

title: What does "28 years of experience" actually mean in practice?
text:
It means I've seen most failure modes before, and more importantly, I've learned what questions to ask before building, not after shipping.

The work spans five distinct engineering roles across 24+ Fortune 500 clients in energy, defense, banking, automotive, healthcare, and aerospace. Each engagement brought a new set of system boundaries, integration constraints, and failure modes. The CSBB Dispatch Framework ran through energy sector clients (FPL, Exelon, NRG, BP). The same cross-domain pattern extended into classified defense environments at General Dynamics Electric Boat. Accessibility methodology tested across global banking at HSBC, PNC, and TD Bank.

What accumulates isn't just technical breadth, it's pattern recognition. The obvious diagnosis is usually incomplete. The first stakeholder framing is usually wrong. The thing that looks like a tracking bug is usually a UI architecture failure. Experience means knowing which questions to ask before you've written a single line of code.

title: What's your actual problem-solving methodology?
text:
I use what I call NTSB-style forensic methodology, the same discipline aviation accident investigators use. Start with the evidence, not the hypothesis. Capture the actual data flow first. Map the system boundaries. Identify where the contract between systems breaks down. Build the evidence chain before you present a diagnosis.

The clearest example: General Motors reported a spike in incomplete course records. Everyone assumed backend tracking bug. I captured HTTP traffic with Fiddler, reverse-engineered the LMS integration, and found something no one expected. SCORM state was held in volatile memory with no autosave. Any browser interruption lost the entire session. Wrong hypothesis, wrong investigation, zero results. Right methodology: trace the data, then talk.

title: How do you decide what to build vs. what to buy?
text:
I evaluate tools by asking whether they solve a real problem I'm already working around, not whether they're popular. The SCORM debugger I built in Vue.js is the cleanest example. For years, diagnosing course tracking failures meant coordinating LMS access with client IT departments. Hours of overhead before investigation could even start.

I built a tool that emulated both SCORM 1.2 and SCORM 2004 runtime environments locally, with real-time API call streaming via the BroadcastChannel API. Diagnostic time: hours to minutes. I chose Vue.js because the reactive data binding matched the live call stream. I chose BroadcastChannel over WebSockets because the tool was purely client-side, no server infrastructure to maintain.

On the MLS Power Platform project, I applied the same logic at a different scale. The recommendation wasn't "adopt new tools," it was "use the platform's existing capabilities properly." Sometimes the right tool is already there.

title: What happens when you don't have all the information upfront?
text:
Missing information is the default state, not an exception. In 28 years of consulting work, I've almost never started a problem with complete information. Clients often don't know what's wrong. Documentation is missing or inaccurate. The person who built the system is long gone.

My approach: build the information I need through systematic investigation. Observe the system. Form a hypothesis from observable behavior. Test the hypothesis against data. Iterate. Don't wait for someone to hand you the answer, go find it.

This same discipline applied when I designed the accessibility testing methodology for HSBC, PNC, and TD Bank. No established framework existed for testing interactive, JavaScript-driven eLearning. I couldn't look it up. So I built it from first principles: WCAG 2.1 as the baseline, ARIA Authoring Practices for interactive patterns, and hands-on JAWS/NVDA testing as the ground truth.

title: How do you ramp up on an unfamiliar technology or codebase quickly?
text:
My pattern: map the platform's boundaries, figure out where the abstractions leak, and build around the constraints. This is the same approach whether it's a new platform, an inherited codebase with no docs, or a protocol I've never worked with before.

On ContentAIQ, I joined an existing React/TypeScript codebase with Jest tests and Azure DevOps CI pipelines and had to get productive fast in their specific patterns and conventions while shipping features alongside the existing team. On MCAPS, I dove into Copilot Studio documentation and started building immediately, learning through the work itself. On Ready2Role, I had a running production system I couldn't read and had to reconstruct from compiled artifacts.

The throughline: trace the data flow, find the boundaries, and build from there. Don't wait until you understand everything. Build something small that tests your assumptions, and let the system tell you where you're wrong.

title: How do you approach cross-system integration? What does that actually look like?
text:
Integration architecture is where most of my career impact has landed. The core discipline: understand each system on its own terms, then find the reliable path between them.

The CSBB Dispatch Framework is the largest example. Energy utilities like FPL, Exelon, NRG, and Calpine each ran different LMS platforms with different SCORM versions. GP Strategies needed one content catalog to work everywhere. I built a SCORM API proxy with a plugin model, separate adapters for SCORM 1.2, SCORM 2004, and AICC, so the proxy detected the client runtime and routed calls to the right adapter. ACL-based tenant isolation meant competing utilities could share hosting infrastructure. The Generator toolchain automated client onboarding. That architecture ran in production for 10+ years across 20+ clients.

I independently arrived at the same architectural pattern Rustici later commercialized as SCORM Dispatch, five years before their product launched.

---

[[FAQ Index]]

title: How do you handle architecture decisions when there are real trade-offs with no clean answer?
text:
I make the trade-off explicit and accept it deliberately. With the CSBB Dispatch Framework, I chose centralization over distribution. A centralized host meant one update reached all 20+ clients simultaneously, version consistency guaranteed. The downside was a single point of failure. I accepted that because the alternative, distributing content to each client, meant losing version control and coordinating bug fixes across 20 separate IT departments with their own change windows.

At AICPA, I saw the other side of the same decision: a webhook-driven sync service where PostgreSQL tracked per-subaccount sync state as an idempotency layer, designed to tolerate upstream schema changes without breaking downstream integrations. Different constraints, different answer.

Same discipline: document the trade-offs in writing, make the constraints visible to everyone, and let each group see why the others' priorities matter.

title: Can you actually rescue a legacy system with no documentation and no original developer?
text:
Yes. It's most of what I did for two decades.

The Ready2Role CMS at GP Strategies is the clearest example. Original developer built a Java/JSP content management system, then left with zero documentation and zero handoff. Running production system. Actively serving client courses. No source repository, just compiled artifacts and a live application.

I decompiled the deployed packages and reconstructed the system's behavior through analysis alone: mapping data flows from the JSP frontend through SQL Server stored procedures, understanding the authoring workflows, identifying which pieces were load-bearing. Where server-side refactoring was impractical, and without documentation or tests, most of it was, I built JavaScript workarounds to extend functionality from the client side.

That system ran in production for 10+ years beyond its expected end of life. It produced roughly 1,200 courses, about 60% of the organization's catalog. Reverse-engineering is not just reading code; it's building a mental model of a system from its observable behavior when no one can tell you how it works.

title: When should you replace a legacy system vs. stabilize it?
text:
The Ready2Role story taught me the most important lesson: sometimes the right answer is not to replace a legacy system but to stabilize it and keep it producing value while you invest new development effort elsewhere.

Replace when: the system can't be understood, can't be safely modified, and its failure modes are unpredictable. Stabilize when: you can understand the failure modes, extension is possible from the outside, and replacement would disrupt more than it solves.

Documentation is the real lesson underneath this. A single developer leaving without a handoff created years of unnecessary risk and technical debt. The cost of that knowledge loss compounded for over a decade. Write the documentation as if the person who needs it most is someone who's never seen the system, because that person is future-you, or it's whoever inherits this from you.

title: How do you work with stakeholders who have conflicting priorities?
text:
I document the trade-offs in writing, make the constraints visible to everyone, and let each group see why the others' priorities matter. The technique that's worked consistently across engagements: don't negotiate between stakeholders, surface the shared constraints so each group understands the others' reality.

The UTC legacy branding project is a clean example. Project leadership wanted speed. The manual review team needed clear criteria. The technical team needed automation that wouldn't produce false positives and undermine trust in the process. I coordinated a multi-disciplinary team, delivered 75% automation coverage, cataloged 450+ branded elements, and got there by having honest conversations about the 25% that required human judgment.

Over seven years at General Dynamics Electric Boat, I sustained 571 documented interactions across IT security, network engineers, LMS administrators, content developers, and project management, by keeping the constraints visible and never asking one group to accept risk that belonged to another.

title: How do you push back on technical decisions you disagree with?
text:
I make the case with evidence, not authority. When I designed the CSBB Dispatch Framework, the conventional approach was a reverse proxy. I argued for client-side cross-domain messaging instead. The disagreement was real, a reverse proxy was the familiar pattern and some people were skeptical that browser-based messaging could handle production SCORM traffic.

I made the case on operational grounds: a reverse proxy would put delivery reliability outside our control, dependent on each client IT department's responsiveness. The client-side approach kept operational complexity on our side.

I didn't just argue the point, I built it and demonstrated that it worked. The framework ran in production for 10+ years across 7+ enterprise energy clients. That track record settled the debate more effectively than any presentation. The pattern is consistent: build the evidence, show the results, let the work speak.

title: What does enterprise accessibility work actually involve beyond running automated tools?
text:
Automated tools catch maybe 30% of real accessibility issues. The rest require using the content the way a screen reader user would.

I designed an enterprise accessibility testing methodology from scratch because the one I needed didn't exist. Existing WCAG guidelines assumed static pages, not interactive, JavaScript-driven content with drag-and-drop, timed assessments, audio players, and branching navigation.

The methodology I built works in three layers: WCAG 2.1 and Section 508 as the baseline (focus management, keyboard navigation, semantic structure, color contrast). ARIA Authoring Practices for the interactive patterns WCAG doesn't cover well, custom widgets, live regions for dynamic content updates, role assignments for non-standard UI components. And hands-on screen reader testing with both JAWS and NVDA as the ground truth.

I applied this across roughly 100 courses for HSBC, PNC, and TD Bank. Colleagues adopted the methodology for their own projects. Accessibility is a design constraint you build around from the start, not a remediation pass at the end.

title: What's your experience with AI-assisted development and LLM integration?
text:
I see AI as a new category of integration problem, connecting language models to structured data, managing state across conversational sessions, building reliable workflows on top of non-deterministic components. Same discipline I've applied to SCORM protocols, webhook-driven sync services, and cross-domain messaging for two decades.

In late 2025, I rebuilt Microsoft's AI-Led Training agent as a Copilot Studio implementation for their MCAPS global sales organization. I'd never worked in Copilot Studio before. The challenge was that the platform sits at an intersection of LLM conversational routing, adaptive cards for deterministic UI rendering, and structured data for managing learner state. I designed a hybrid architecture that used each technology for what it does well, adaptive cards for structured interactions where you need predictable UI, the LLM layer for conversational routing where natural language makes sense.

The tools change. The engineering discipline of tracing data flow, understanding failure modes, and building reliable boundaries between systems stays the same.
